skillList.name: Muscle Up
skillList.description:
The Muscle-Up can be divided into 3 phases:
1. Pull
2. Transition
3. Push
First you pull your body as high as you can (chest or even hip on level with bar). The probably toughest part is the transition. To succeed you'll need to rotate your wrists on top of the bar and throw your shoulders over the bar. The last step is a bar Dip.
The Muscle-up needs almost all muscles in your upper body. It needs explosivness and a good coordination of the muscles. The Muscle-up can also be performed with gymnastic rings. On the rings you'll often see a false grip (start dead hang with wrists over rings).

progressions.name: Pull Up
progressions.workoutDescription: Build basic upper body strength as foundation for the Muscle Up.

progressions.name: High Pull Up
progressions.workoutDescription: Increase explosiveness and height of Pull Ups. Specialize Dip strength for Muscle Up.

progressions.name: Jumping Muscle Up
progressions.workoutDescription: Improve the Muscle Up transition and increase strength for this specific phase.

plan.name: Jumping Muscle Up
plan.workoutDescription:
Execution: Find a lower bar or something you can jump of. Put your feet below the bar and grab it with a bit more than shoulder wide apart grip. Pull yourself up by retracting your scapula and pulling with your back and arm muscles. Additionally help the upward movement through jumping up. You shouldn't use all your force from your legs. They are only meant as help to get enough height to start the transition.

You can decrease the power from your legs by not putting your feeting directly under the bar, but some steps behind.

Quickly tighten your abs, pull the bar to your body and throw your shoulder over the bar.

Notice that is very important to rotate your wrists during the transition in order to get them above the bar, otherwise you will stuck

plan.name: High Pull Up
plan.workoutDescription: Execution: Start in a dead hang position. Perform a regular Pull Up and pause at the top. Explosively push yourself back while trying to get your arms straight, but remaining at the same height. Quickly pull yourself back to the top Pull Up position.

progressions.name: Kipping Muscle Up
progressions.workoutDescription: Work on the entire Muscle up movement with the help of momentum.

progressions.name: Muscle Up
progressions.workoutDescription: Increase your max Muscle Up reps.

skillList.name: Planche
skillList.description:
The Planche is one of the most impressive Calisthenic skills and requires a lot of wrist, straight arm and shoulder strength. In a Planche you hold your body parallel to the ground with your legs in the air. Lean far forward (hands below hip) in order to keep your balance. Your arms should be straight and your elbows locked out. Your scapula needs to protracted, so push your shoulder down. With a tightened core and a hollow body keep your body in a position where it is parallel to the ground.
Hand placement: You can use different hand placement (fingers forward, fingers to the side, fingertips hold, fingers backward). Use the one you feel most comfortable with, but keep attention to don't overstress your wrists (you can reduce the stress on your writs by using parallettes or push up bars).
Recommended Read:
* Gold Medal Bodies - Planche
* Functionally Mad - Lets Planche
* Rough Strength - How to achieve Planche
* My Planche Traning

skillList.name: Front Lever
skillList.description: Execution: The Front Lever is an advanced Calisthenics skill, where you hold the back of your body parallel to the ground, supported only through your arms. Your arms should be straight and your core tightened. Retract your scapula (push shoulders back) and keep your hips high (on level with shoulders).

progressions.name: Tuck Front Lever
progressions.workoutDescription: Learn straight arm pull strength and scapula retraction.

progressions.name: Adv. Tuck Front Lever
progressions.workoutDescription: Increase straight arm pull strength.

progressions.name: Front Lever
progressions.workoutDescription: Improve Front Lever hold times.

skillList.name: Back Lever
skillList.description: The Back Lever is an advanced Calisthenics skill, where you hold the back of your body parallel to the ground, supported only through your arms. Keep your arms straight and protract your scapula by pushing your shoulders forward. The Back Lever starts in a dead hang position. Pull your legs to the bar and continue rotating them past your elbows. Now increase the leverage through raising your legs up, while keeping straight and tight. Stop the rotation when your back is parallel to the ground, and hold yourself in this position. Keep your arms straight and protract your scapula by pushing your shoulders forward

progressions.name: Skin the Cat
progressions.workoutDescription: Increase shoulder mobility and straight arm push strength.

skillList.name: Pistol Squat
skillList.description: The Pistol Squat is a great exercise to increase your leg strength and power. It will also improve your balance and vertical jump. The Pistol squat is performed by standing on one leg. Descend slowly and balanced while keeping your weight on your heel and reaching your other leg into the air. The bottom position is reached when your butt almost touches your heel. Pause briefly then squat up, but keep attention to turn your knee outwards und push from your heel. During the whole move keep the tension in your abs for better balancing. A focused an correct performance of the Pistol Squat is very important. If you feel pain in your knees you should stop perform it or get advice in order to correct your technique. You should always train both sides.

skillList.name: Handstand Push Up
skillList.description: The handstand push up combines push strength and balance. Start in a Handstand and descend controlled by keeping a straight body and leaning forward (a hollow body needs more strength than a arched back). Before your nose touches the ground push yourself up by keeping your elbows inwards. One rep counts if it was performed in full range of motion (nose almost touching ground & open shoulders in handstand position)

skillList.name: V-Sit
skillList.description:
The V-Sit demands 3 things:
1. Support strength
2. Abs strength
3. Hamstring flexibility
The support strength is needed to raise from the ground through pushing with your arms and shoulders (scapula depression). So if you think your arms are to short to perform this skill, maybe you just doesn't depress your scapula enough. Next you need strong abs to lift your legs from the ground. The most leverage to lift will be with straight legs. The more flexible hamstrings you have, the easier it will be to extend your legs. In order to get from the L-Sit to the V-Sit you need to push your hips forward and up.

skillList.name: One Arm Pull Up
skillList.description:
The One Arm Pull Up is a display of massive pulling power.

Precondition: You should have at least 15 clean Pull Ups before starting to train for the One Arm Pull Up.

Execution: Start in an One Arm Dead Hang by gripping the bar with one hand. Keep tension in your torso to avoid rotating. Start the movement by pulling your shoulder blade down (this is also called Active One Arm Hang or One Arm Scapula Shrug). Bend your arm and pull yourself up with your back muscles and biceps. The aim is to touch the bar with the inactive shoulder.

skillList.name: Human Flag
skillList.description: The Human Flag is usually done on vertical or wall bars, hereby the body is hold parallel to the ground only supported by the arms. Your lower arm presses into the anchor point, while your upper arm helps you balance your tightened body. The most important muscles in this skill are your shoulders, because there will be the highest force of gravity. To avoid disbalances you should always train both sides

skillList.name: Dragon Flag
skillList.description:
The Dragon Flag is one of the most advanced Core Exercises and became very popular through Bruce Lee and the Rocky movie.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The movement starts in a top position, so swing yourself up. Your weight should now rest on your shoulders.

Step 2: Now tense your whole body and bring it into a hollow body position. Slowly lower yourself while keeping the hollow body position.

Step 3: Before you touch the ground pull yourself back up and repeat. Keep attention to don't break the tension in your core. You have to keep the hollow body position, otherwise you will hurt your back. You can decrease the difficulty be tucking your legs in or by reducing the range of motion.
